pr.title: Match some large multiman init functions
pr.author: r-burns

summary: Matched five large gmmultiman init functions by replacing placeholder stubs with C implementations and tightening related multiman data/prototype types. The decomp.dev bot reported +4584 matched bytes, +0.12% matched code, with gm_801B7154, gm_801B7700, gm_801B7C84, gm_801B8220, and gm_801B874C going from 0.00% to 100.00%. Evidence is mostly from the diff and bot match report; there was no PR body or human review discussion.

key_files.role: Main matching work: implemented five previously stubbed multiman init functions and added a small helper for multiman VsModeData access.
Added struct UnkMultimanData with bitfield/member layout used by the matched functions.
Added forward declaration typedef for UnkMultimanData.
Replaced several UNK_PARAMS prototypes with typed signatures needed by the new gmmultiman code.

what_changed: Implemented gm_801B7154, gm_801B7700, gm_801B7C84, gm_801B8220, and gm_801B874C in src/melee/gm/gmmultiman.c, replacing address-stub comments.

Each new init routine builds StartMeleeData from MinorScene via gm_801A427C, copies rules and six player entries from gmMainLib_804D3EE0->unk_1490, initializes player data with gm_80167A14/gm_801B0620, flips opponent teams, disables xC_b1 for non-player entries, and calls gm_8016F088.
Mode-specific setup uses mode IDs 0x22, 0x23, 0x24, 0x25, and 0x26 with gm_80182554 and gm_80182DF0, then records per-character/mode values through gm_80181A44, gm_80181AC8, and gm_80181B64.
Added static inline getMultimanData returning &gmMainLib_804D3EE0->unk_1490 for repeated multiman data access.
Added UnkMultimanData in types.h with fields u16 x0_0 : 1, u16 x2, and int x4.
Added UnkMultimanData forward typedef in forward.h.

Updated gm_17C0.h prototypes from UNK_PARAMS to typed parameters/return where used: gm_80181A44(int c_kind, int, bool), gm_80181AC8(int, int, int), gm_80181B64(int c_kind, int, u32), gm_80182554(int, int), and gm_80182DF0(int c_kind, int) returning UnkMultimanData*.
Removed local extern declarations for gm_804D68E8, gm_804D68E9, gm_804D68F0, and gm_804D68F1 from gmmultiman.c in the shown diff.
decomp.dev reported five new matches in main/melee/gm/gmmultiman totaling +4584 bytes.
smart_moves: Used a small typed UnkMultimanData struct instead of leaving gm_80182DF0 as an opaque UNK_RET, allowing the matched functions to express x0_0, x2, and x4 writes directly.
Tightened function prototypes in gm_17C0.h before relying on them from the newly matched code, reducing casts/UNK_PARAMS pressure and documenting inferred argument roles such as c_kind.

Recognized the shared init skeleton across several multiman variants and reused the same player/rules setup pattern while only changing mode-specific rule fields, timers, score pointers, and mode IDs.
Preserved matching-sensitive stack behavior with PAD_STACK(8) in functions where needed.
Introduced getMultimanData only for repeated access to gmMainLib_804D3EE0->unk_1490, keeping the abstraction minimal and still close to the generated code shape.
decomp_lessons: Large game-mode init functions often become tractable by identifying the repeated skeleton first: fetch StartMeleeData, copy VsModeData rules/players, normalize player/team bits, then perform mode-specific stat/high-score setup.
When an unknown helper returns a struct-like region that is immediately field-written, defining a narrow Unk* struct with only proven fields can unlock matches without overnaming unknown semantics.
Header prototype cleanup can be part of the matching tactic, not just cleanup: replacing UNK_PARAMS with inferred parameter widths/types helps the compiler generate the right calls.
Mode IDs and constants should be kept literal when semantics are not proven; this PR used 0x22 through 0x26 and rule timer values like 0xB4 and 0x384 without inventing names.
Bitfields such as x0_0 may be necessary for layout/codegen fidelity when the assembly is testing or storing a boolean-like subfield.
For repeated player array setup, preserving loop bounds and order matters: this code consistently copies six players, then mutates players 1 through 5.
naming_conventions: Unknown-but-typed structs use the project style UnkNameData, here UnkMultimanData.
Unidentified fields retain offset/bitfield-style names such as x0_0, x2, x4, x0_6, x0_7, x5_0, x5_1, xB, xC_b1, and xD_b4.
Address-based function names remain for not-yet-semanticized game-mode functions, e.g. gm_801B7154 and gm_80182DF0.
The prototype used c_kind as a named argument where the value is clearly a character kind, while other uncertain integer parameters were left unnamed.
Local variables in matched code retain decompiler/register-style names such as temp_r31, temp_r30, temp_r29, and temp_r3, indicating matching priority over readability cleanup.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used PAD_STACK(8) in gm_801B7154, gm_801B7C84, gm_801B8220, and gm_801B874C to satisfy stack/layout matching requirements shown in the diff.
Preserved explicit loops over i < 6 and i = 1; i < 6 for player copy and opponent mutation rather than abstracting them away.
Kept boolean conversions explicit in places like temp_r28->x0_0 = *temp_r27 ? true : false to match codegen.
Used typed pointer-return helpers such as gm_80182DF0 returning UnkMultimanData* to express field stores that likely correspond to direct offset writes.
Maintained mode-specific call order: gm_80182554, retrieve saved stat pointer, gm_80182DF0, set UnkMultimanData fields, then call gm_80181A44/gm_80181AC8/gm_80181B64 as applicable.
Matched very similar functions by varying only constants and stat accessors: gmMainLib_8015D6F8, gmMainLib_8015D74C, gmMainLib_8015D7A4, gmMainLib_8015D7BC, and gmMainLib_8015D7D4.

review_feedback.feedback: GALE01 report showed matched code increased to 38.01%, +0.12%, +4584 bytes, with five new 100% matches in main/melee/gm/gmmultiman.
No human review comments were present in the provided PR slice.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rename or further type UnkMultimanData fields x0_0, x2, and x4.
Search for additional gmmultiman init stubs near gm_801B70DC, gm_801B74F0, gm_801B7688, gm_801B7AA0, gm_801B7C0C, gm_801B8024, gm_801B81A8, gm_801B8580, gm_801B86D4, and gm_801B8AF8.
Look for PRs that give semantic names to mode IDs 0x22 through 0x26.
Compare other game-mode init functions that copy gmMainLib_804D3EE0->unk_1490 into StartMeleeData to reuse the same matching skeleton.
Check whether gm_80181A44, gm_80181AC8, gm_80181B64, gm_80182554, and gm_80182DF0 later receive semantic names or more precise parameter names.
